phenomenon: how linguistic form shapes interpersonal judgment
claim: describing ongoing action (vs. completed action) increases inference of intentional agency through richer mental simulation.
working memory integrates individual items with aggregate summary statistics, biasing individual retrieval toward ensemble properties.
gustatory perception of bitterness induces moral disgust, with stronger effect in politically conservative individuals.

phenomenon: bodily purity priming and moral/political judgment
claim: reminders of physical cleanliness shift moral standards toward conservatism and sexual-purity severity.
death-related thoughts trigger self-defense in independent-self cultures but other-affirmation in interdependent-self cultures.

phenomenon: incidental environmental cue and durable political preference shift
claim: brief incidental exposure to a national symbol shifts political beliefs and behavior durably, despite awareness denial.

phenomenon: strategy selection under emotional intensity variation
claim: low-intensity distress favors engagement (reappraisal); high-intensity distress favors disengagement (distraction).

phenomenon: race-moderated backlash against agentic female leadership
claim: dominance-backlash against agentic women occurs for White women but not Black women, showing race-gender intersection.
respect and standing in face-to-face groups predicts well-being more strongly than economic status.
priming the concept of choice activates belief in individual agency and reduces concern about wealth inequality.
sexualized women are perceived analytically (like objects) rather than configurally (like persons), inverted-inversion-effect.
liberals and conservatives moralize environment differently; reframing environmental harm as purity-violation reduces partisan gap.
memory-test contexts make older adults feel older through cognitive-challenge stereotype activation, sparing younger adults.
women object to sexual imagery more when linked to low-value products; high-value association mitigates objection.
racial progress increases anti-White-bias perceptions, but only among Whites who view status hierarchy as legitimate.
salience of increasing racial diversity shifts White political affiliation and policy positions toward conservatism via threat.

phenomenon: free-will belief and moral-responsibility judgment in punishment
claim: weakening free-will beliefs and scientific-neuroscience framing reduce retributive punishment support.
high-status individuals produce distinct acoustic patterns (higher pitch variability, greater loudness variability) that perceivers use to infer rank.
having weak alternatives creates low-value anchors that depress first offers; having no alternatives is liberating.
reminders of God increase nonmoral risk-taking by increasing perceived safety, especially for believers in divine protection.
